lower.effect: white remains runny and partially translucent

upper.effect: yolk begins to set, becomes creamy but not liquid

condition: when you want fully set white with liquid, runny yolk
decision: Cook egg at exactly 63°C for 45 minutes

failure_state.result: white not set, unsafe for consumption
yolk partially set, loses runny texture

effect: Ovotransferrin denatures at 61-62°C setting white; ovalbumin requires higher temperature, allowing yolk to remain liquid at 63°C

mechanism: myosin denaturation and myoglobin redox state

lower.effect: rare: deep red, very soft, myosin minimally denatured

upper.effect: medium: pink center, firmer, collagen begins contraction

condition: when aiming for medium-rare steak with pink center and high juiciness
decision: Cook beef at 55°C for 1-4 hours depending on thickness

failure_state.result: rare or blue rare; may be too soft for preference
loss of pink color; moisture loss increases significantly

effect: Myosin denatures around 50-55°C; myoglobin denatures at higher temperatures, determining color; collagen begins contracting above 55°C

sensory.appearance: pink center, brown edges after searing

ingredient_state: tough cut with high collagen (brisket, short ribs)

lower.effect: collagen breakdown extremely slow; meat remains tough

upper.effect: risk of meat becoming mushy as muscle fibers completely break down

condition: when cooking tough cuts to achieve shreddable, fork-tender texture
decision: Cook at 85°C for 8-24 hours until collagen fully converts
effect: Collagen hydrolyzes to gelatin at 70-90°C; conversion rate doubles every 10°C increase; gelatin adds mouthfeel and perceived moisture

sensory.juiciness: high from rendered fat and gelatin

mechanism: myosin denaturation and muscle fiber separation

upper.effect: firmer, flakes more distinctly, less silky

condition: when aiming for medium-rare salmon with silky, custard-like texture

failure_state.result: fish becomes flaky and loses silky texture

effect: Myosin denatures at 45-50°C; higher temperatures cause actin denaturation and moisture expulsion

lower.effect: texture is very soft, almost raw-like, safety concerns

upper.effect: fibers become dry and stringy, moisture loss accelerates

condition: when aiming for tender, juicy chicken breast
decision: Cook chicken breast at 60°C for 1-2 hours
effect: Myosin denatures at 50-55°C; actin denatures at 66-70°C; cooking at 60°C sets myosin while actin remains undenatured, preserving moisture

mechanism: hydrogen bond disruption and granule swelling

upper.effect: full gelatinization; risk of scorching if not stirred

condition: when thickening sauces, gravies, or puddings with starch
decision: Heat starch-liquid mixture to 85°C while stirring until thickened
effect: Starch granules absorb water and swell between 60-90°C, releasing amylose and creating viscosity; cornstarch gelatinizes at lower temperature than flour

sensory.appearance: translucent to opaque depending on starch type

condition: when making mayonnaise, hollandaise, or vinaigrette
decision: Add oil slowly to water phase while mixing vigorously; use emulsifier (egg yolk, mustard, lecithin)
effect: Emulsifiers reduce surface tension, allowing fat droplets to remain suspended in water; slow incorporation creates smaller droplets for more stable emulsion

mechanism: granule swelling and cell wall rupture

lower.effect: starch not fully gelatinized; potatoes remain firm

upper.effect: cell walls rupture; potatoes become mushy

equipment: pot of water or sous vide
condition: when cooking potatoes for mashing or tender texture
decision: Cook potatoes in water at 85°C until fork-tender
effect: Starch gelatinizes at 60-80°C; pectin in cell walls breaks down at higher temperatures; cooking at 85°C balances tenderness with structure

upper.effect: caramel burns, becomes bitter and black

condition: when making caramel sauce or candy
decision: Heat sugar without stirring until melted and amber-colored; add liquid carefully
effect: Sugar decomposes above 150°C, creating hundreds of flavor compounds; crystallization occurs if crystals seed the melt

sensory.texture: fluid when hot, brittle when cool

lower.effect: beta V crystals do not form; chocolate blooms

upper.effect: beta V crystals melt; temper lost

condition: when making chocolate coatings or molded chocolates
decision: Heat dark chocolate to 45-50°C to melt all crystals, cool to 27-28°C while stirring to form beta V crystals, reheat to 31-32°C for working

failure_state.result: bloom (white streaks), poor snap, difficulty unmolding

effect: Cocoa butter forms six crystal types; only beta V gives proper snap, gloss, and shrinkage for unmolding
